basic_info.name: Excipient Compatibility Screening
basic_info.name_ja: 賦形剤適合性スクリーニング
basic_info.description: Excipient compatibility screening uses HSP distance between an active pharmaceutical ingredient (API) and common tablet excipients (binders, fillers, disintegrants, lubricants, glidants) to predict physical stability of solid dosage formulations. A small HSP distance (Ra) between API and excipient indicates thermodynamic affinity, which may promote dissolution of the drug into the excipient matrix or eutectic formation, while a large Ra suggests immiscibility and physical separation. By mapping all candidate excipients in Hansen space relative to the API, formulators can rapidly identify combinations prone to incompatibility (e.g., API softening, discoloration, potency loss) and prioritize stable formulations before committing to accelerated stability studies.

hsp_principle.hsp_principle: The physical compatibility between an API and excipients is governed by the thermodynamic affinity of their molecular interactions. When the HSP distance (Ra) between the API and an excipient is small, the components share similar dispersion, polar, and hydrogen-bonding characteristics, which can lead to molecular-level mixing, solid solution formation, or surface wetting effects that alter drug stability or dissolution. Conversely, a large Ra indicates dissimilar interaction profiles and predicts physical separation with minimal interaction. For excipient screening, the goal is context-dependent: for binders, moderate affinity may be desirable for granule cohesion, while for lubricants like magnesium stearate, excessive affinity to the API surface can retard dissolution. The approach positions the API and all candidate excipients as points in 3D Hansen space and uses Ra or RED values to rank compatibility risk.
hsp_principle.typical_red_threshold: Ra < 5 MPa^0.5 suggests high affinity (risk of solid-state interaction); Ra > 10 MPa^0.5 suggests low interaction risk; intermediate values require case-by-case evaluation

required_inputs: Excipient HSP values (dD, dP, dH) for each candidate
Temperature of storage and processing conditions
data_sources: HSPiP database for common pharmaceutical excipients
Group contribution methods (Van Krevelen, Hoftyzer) for novel excipients
Handbook of Pharmaceutical Excipients for physical property data
Published literature values for FDA-approved excipients (e.g., MCC, lactose, PVP, HPMC, magnesium stearate)
Experimental solubility testing for HSP determination of new APIs
complementary_parameters: Melting point and glass transition temperature of API and excipients
pH of the microenvironment (for pH-sensitive interactions)

limitations: HSP predicts thermodynamic affinity but not kinetic rates of solid-state reactions or chemical degradation
Chemical incompatibilities (e.g., Maillard reaction between lactose and primary amines) are not captured by HSP distance
Surface interactions at particle-particle contacts may differ from bulk HSP predictions
Moisture-mediated interactions add complexity not fully described by anhydrous HSP values
Polymorphic form of the API can shift effective HSP values
Mechanical processing (milling, compaction) can alter local interactions beyond equilibrium HSP predictions
known_failure_modes: Chemical reactions between API and excipient functional groups (acid-base, oxidation, transesterification) not predicted by HSP
Moisture-catalyzed degradation where water acts as a mediator rather than a solvent
Lubricant over-blending effects that are process-dependent rather than thermodynamic
Excipient impurities (e.g., residual peroxides in PEG, formaldehyde in PVP) driving incompatibility
validation_method: Binary mixture accelerated stability studies (40C/75%RH, 2-4 weeks)
Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) of API-excipient blends for thermal event shifts
Isothermal stress testing (IST) with HPLC assay for degradation products
XRPD for polymorphic changes in blends

key_references.author: Greenhalgh, D.J. et al.
key_references.title: Solubility parameters as predictors of miscibility in solid dispersions

key_references.author: Hancock, B.C. et al.
key_references.title: The use of solubility parameters in pharmaceutical dosage form design

key_references.author: Bharate, S.S. et al.
key_references.title: Interactions and incompatibilities of pharmaceutical excipients with active pharmaceutical ingredients: a comprehensive review

key_references.author: Forster, A. et al.
key_references.title: Selection of excipients for melt extrusion with two poorly water-soluble drugs by solubility parameter calculation and thermal analysis

literature.regulatory_relevance: Excipient compatibility screening is a standard component of ICH Q8 pharmaceutical development. HSP-based rationale for excipient selection is accepted in IND and NDA filings as supporting evidence for formulation design. FDA guidance on pharmaceutical development encourages science-based approaches to excipient selection, and HSP screening is recognized as a valid in-silico preselection tool.

commercial_tools: Minitab / JMP for DOE-based compatibility studies
